common.welcome: The goal of this survey is to find out the impact of a Social Income for you. Takes around 5 minutes to complete.

questions.hasDependentsDescV1: People who depend financially on you.
questions.nrDependentsTitleV1: How many people depend on you financially?
questions.employmentStatusTitleV1: What is your current employment status?
questions.notEmployedTitleV1: Are you currently looking for work?
questions.disabilityTitleV1: Do you have a disability or illness that affects your possibilities of getting work or keeping your job?
questions.skippingMealsTitleV1: Do you or other family members in your household ever skip meals because there wasn't enough money for food?
questions.skippingMealsLastWeek3MealsTitleV1: Did you skip more than 3 meals in the past week?
questions.unexpectedExpensesCoveredTitleV1: Do you have enough money to cover unexpected expenses like seeing a doctor?
questions.unexpectedExpensesCoveredDescV1: A doctor costing for example 100 SLE or above.
questions.savingsTitleV1: Are you currently able to save money?
questions.debtPersonalRepayTitleV1: Are you able to repay the debt?
questions.debtHouseholdTitleV1: Is anyone in your household in debt?
questions.debtHouseholdWhoRepaysTitleV1: Are you the main person repaying the debt?
questions.otherSupportTitleV1: Are you currently receiving any other form of financial support from anywhere else?
questions.plannedAchievementTitleV1: What do you hope to achieve within the next 3 years with Social Income?
questions.spendingTitleV1: What are the key categories that you spend your Social Income payments on?
questions.spendingDescV1: Education: school fees, stationary, textbooks etc. // Food for you or your household // Housing: rent, house maintenance etc. // Health care: doctor, hospital visit, medicine etc. // Transportation: bus, taxi, poda poda, keke etc. // Saving: on bank account or mobile money account etc. // Investment: fertilizers, tools, diesel for machinery etc.
questions.spendingRankingTitleV1: Please rank the selected spending categories from the most important to the least.
questions.spendingRankingDescV1: You can drag and drop the categories.
questions.plannedAchievementRemainingTitleV1: What do you hope to achieve within the remaining time in the Social Income program?
questions.financialIndependenceTitleV1: Did Social Income make you feel financially more secure and independent?
questions.impactLifeGeneralTitleV1: What impact did Social Income have on your life?
questions.longEnoughTitleV1: Was three years of Social Income payments long enough to become financially stable?
questions.achievementsAchievedTitleV1: Have you achieved what you hoped to over the course of the last three years?
questions.achievementsNotAchievedCommentTitleV1: What has kept you from achieving this goal? Is there something we could have done to further support you? (e.g. mentoring, connections)
questions.happierTitleV1: Has Social Income made you feel happier?

impactInsights.spendingV1: Food spending remains a pressure point, which helps explain why recipients often prioritize short-term household stability over longer-horizon investments.
impactInsights.hasDependentsV1: Many recipients support others, so one transfer often stabilizes more than one person. This suggests program effects are likely to extend beyond the direct recipient.
impactInsights.debtPersonalV1: Personal debt appears to be used as a coping mechanism during income shocks, indicating that regular transfers can reduce reliance on high-stress borrowing cycles.
impactInsights.debtHouseholdV1: Household debt dynamics suggest financial stress is often shared within families, not only individual. That supports framing impact at household level, not recipient level alone.
impactInsights.employmentStatusV1: Self-employment patterns align with informal labor realities where income is often irregular. Transfers can function more as working-capital smoothing than wage replacement.
impactInsights.skippingMealsV1: Meal-skipping responses indicate food insecurity is episodic for many households, pointing to vulnerability around weekly cash-flow gaps rather than only chronic scarcity.
impactInsights.savingsV1: Limited ability to save suggests most transfer value is absorbed by immediate essentials, which is expected in high-inflation contexts and should inform realistic expectations around capital accumulation.
impactInsights.unexpectedExpensesCoveredV1: Difficulty covering unexpected costs highlights fragile resilience. Health or emergency expenses likely remain a key pathway into debt even when regular support exists.
impactInsights.schoolAttendanceV1: School-attendance responses suggest education constraints are not only fee-related and may include caregiving pressure, transport barriers, or opportunity costs within households.
impactInsights.impactFinancialIndependenceV1: Perceived financial independence is improving for many respondents, but this should be interpreted as increased day-to-day control rather than full economic security.
impactInsights.longEnough: Views on whether support duration is sufficient suggest that stability timelines differ across households; fixed program duration may fit some recipients better than others.
impactInsights.plannedAchievementRemainingV1: Open responses indicate recipients are still goal-oriented near later program stages, which points to meaningful agency and planning even under constrained conditions.
impactInsights.impactLifeGeneralV1: Life-impact narratives suggest effects beyond income, including reduced stress and improved decision space, which are important outcomes not captured by financial indicators alone.

impactMeasurement.title: Impact Measurement
impactMeasurement.description: All data shown on this page is updated in real time from surveys conducted with our recipients.
impactMeasurement.loadError: Unable to load survey insights right now.
impactMeasurement.textResponsePrivacyTooltip: Free-text answers are not published for privacy reasons, as they may identify individuals or reveal who is behind a response.
